last_digest.title: China Uses Dual-Method Cyberattack on Czech Orgs
last_digest.rss_summary: China is stealing data from high-value targets via a sneaky, double-layer spear-phishing campaign that includes the Azureveil malware.

last_digest.title: Russia claims foreign spy agencies hacked officials' phones
last_digest.rss_summary: In a statement, Russia's Federal Security Service (FSB) said it had uncovered what it described as a "large-scale operation" involving malicious software installed on the mobile devices of senior Russian officials.

last_digest.title: VS Code zero-day lets hackers steal GitHub tokens in one click
last_digest.rss_summary: A security researcher has released exploit code for a Visual Studio Code (VS Code) zero-day vulnerability that allows attackers to steal GitHub authentication tokens by tricking users into clicking a link. [...]

last_digest.title: Over 116,000 Minecraft systems infected in WeedHack malware campaign
last_digest.rss_summary: A large-scale malware campaign dubbed WeedHack is targeting Minecraft players and has infected more than 116,000 systems since January. [...]

last_digest.title: Supply Chain Attack Hits 32 Red Hat NPM Packages
last_digest.rss_summary:
Hackers published 96 malicious package versions, injected with a credential-stealing worm similar to Mini Shai-Hulud.
The post Supply Chain Attack Hits 32 Red Hat NPM Packages appeared first on SecurityWeek.

last_week_digest.title: Lazarus APT unveils fileless remote access Trojan designed to evade detection
last_week_digest.tldr: The North Korea-linked Lazarus group has deployed a sophisticated, memory-only remote access trojan, enabling long-term espionage without leaving forensic filesystem traces. By utilising Windows Data Protection API and disabling security hooks, the malware evades detection across multiple global financial organisations. This shift towards surgical, fileless toolsets signals a maturing threat landscape where signature-based defences are increasingly obsolete, forcing businesses to prioritise advanced behavioural monitoring and robust incident response capabilities.

last_week_digest.title: Third-Party Cyberattack Impacts Patient Information at The Oncology Institute
last_week_digest.tldr: The Oncology Institute recently confirmed a data breach originating from a third-party software vendor, potentially compromising sensitive patient information. This incident follows a broader security failure at TriZetto, which previously exposed the records of 3.4 million individuals. Such events highlight the systemic risks posed by interconnected supply chains in global healthcare, demonstrating how a single vendor vulnerability can create widespread operational consequences and significant regulatory compliance challenges.

last_week_digest.title: 7-Eleven data breach exposes personal information of 185,000 people
last_week_digest.tldr: The ShinyHunters extortion gang recently breached 7-Eleven, exposing the personal information of 185,300 individuals, including names, dates of birth, and contact details. By targeting the company’s Salesforce environment, attackers compromised sensitive franchisee documentation. This incident highlights the growing vulnerability of corporate cloud platforms to large-scale data theft. It signals a persistent trend where global retailers face significant operational and reputational risks from sophisticated groups exploiting third-party software configurations.

last_week_digest.title: Over 5,500 GitHub Repositories Infected in ‘Megalodon’ Supply Chain Attack
last_week_digest.tldr: The Megalodon supply chain attack compromised over 5,500 GitHub repositories by injecting malicious automated commits into CI workflows to harvest sensitive credentials, tokens, and encryption keys. This breach forces organisations to confront significant operational risks, as compromised secrets facilitate lateral movement and downstream supply chain exploitation. Such incidents signal a growing industry trend where attackers leverage automated CI/CD pipelines to bypass traditional perimeter defences at scale globally.

last_week_digest.title: Anthropic to release Mythos-class models to the public
last_week_digest.tldr: Anthropic’s Mythos AI has identified 23,019 software vulnerabilities, including 6,202 high-severity flaws across critical open-source infrastructure. While expanding access to governments, the company admits current safeguards cannot prevent misuse. This capability creates significant operational strain, as maintainers struggle to patch the deluge of AI-generated reports. The industry must now prepare for an era where automated vulnerability discovery accelerates the weaponisation of flaws by malicious global actors.

last_week_digest.title: Iranian APT Targets Aviation, Software Companies With Updated Tools
last_week_digest.tldr: Iranian state-sponsored threat actor Nimbus Manticore is actively targeting global aviation and software organisations using sophisticated, updated toolsets to bypass security defences. These persistent cyber espionage campaigns threaten critical operational continuity and intellectual property integrity for international firms. This activity signals a broader trend of state actors maintaining aggressive digital operations despite geopolitical pressures, forcing global enterprises to prioritise advanced threat hunting and robust infrastructure resilience strategies.

last_week_digest.title: Charter confirms data breach after ShinyHunters extortion threat
last_week_digest.tldr: Charter Communications confirmed a data breach after the ShinyHunters extortion group claimed to have stolen 40 million customer records via a vishing attack on an employee’s account. While Charter denies sensitive data exfiltration, the incident highlights the growing risk of social engineering targeting corporate SSO and SaaS platforms like Salesforce. This trend signals that global enterprises face escalating threats from sophisticated actors exploiting cloud-based service integrations.

last_week_digest.title: The Hidden Ransomware Economy Running on Exposed Databases
last_week_digest.tldr: A five-year study reveals that 30,515 exposed databases were compromised, affecting over 215 billion records across global systems like MongoDB and MySQL. Despite attackers earning only $753,000, the operational damage remains severe as data is frequently wiped or stolen regardless of payment. This trend signals that for exposed infrastructure, compromise is no longer a probability but a certainty, highlighting an urgent need for robust, automated security configurations worldwide.

last_week_digest.title: Nimbus Manticore Expanded Attacks With AI-Assisted Malware and Fake Zoom Installers
last_week_digest.tldr: The Iran-linked threat actor Nimbus Manticore has escalated global cyber operations against aviation and software sectors across the Middle East, Europe, and Australia. By deploying AI-assisted malware like the MiniFast backdoor and sophisticated trojanized Zoom installers, the group demonstrated rapid tactical adaptation during geopolitical conflict. This shift signals a concerning industry trend where state-sponsored actors leverage generative AI to accelerate development cycles and bypass traditional security defences effectively.

last_week_digest.title: Dutch authorities arrest men suspected of providing infrastructure for Russian cyber operations
last_week_digest.tldr: Dutch authorities arrested two IT entrepreneurs for allegedly providing infrastructure to support Russian cyberattacks and disinformation campaigns, resulting in the seizure of over 800 servers. By circumventing European sanctions, these suspects facilitated destabilising activities against government agencies. This operation highlights the critical role of hosting providers in geopolitical conflict and signals that regulators are increasingly targeting the technical intermediaries that enable state-sponsored digital interference and malicious operations.

last_week_digest.title: LA Metro Cyberattack Linked to Iranian State-Sponsored Hackers
last_week_digest.tldr: Iranian state-sponsored actors have been linked to a significant cyberattack against the Los Angeles Metro, exposing critical transport infrastructure vulnerabilities. While initially attributed to hacktivists, the involvement of sophisticated nation-state infrastructure highlights the escalating risk to public transit systems globally. This incident signals a dangerous shift where essential municipal services face targeted disruption, forcing international organisations to re-evaluate their operational resilience against increasingly coordinated and politically motivated digital threats.

last_week_digest.title: CrowdStrike disrupts Glassworm botnet that preyed on open-source supply chain
last_week_digest.tldr: CrowdStrike, alongside Google and Shadowserver, dismantled the Glassworm botnet, which compromised over 300 GitHub repositories and numerous open-source packages since early 2025. By targeting developer workflows, the attackers threatened global software supply chains across Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. This operation signals a shift toward proactive infrastructure disruption, forcing adversaries to incur significant costs and proving that collaborative, non-judicial interventions are essential for securing modern digital ecosystems.

last_week_digest.title: GPU mining malware spreads via SEO poisoning, AI chatbots
last_week_digest.tldr: Threat actors are exploiting SEO poisoning and AI chatbot recommendations to distribute GPU mining malware, specifically targeting high-performance computing systems. By bundling malicious DLLs with legitimate utility software, attackers gain persistent remote access via ScreenConnect to facilitate cryptojacking. This campaign signals a shift towards high-yield monetization strategies, highlighting how AI-generated search results can be weaponised to bypass traditional security awareness and compromise enterprise-grade hardware infrastructure globally.

last_week_digest.title: The LA Metro Attack Wasn’t Hacktivism. It Was a State Operation With a Costume On.
last_week_digest.tldr: The Iranian-linked group Ababil of Minab recently compromised the Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority, wiping hundreds of terabytes of data and disrupting internal operations for weeks. Forensic evidence confirms this was a state-sponsored intelligence operation disguised as hacktivism. This incident signals a growing trend where nation-states utilise consumer AI tools and deceptive personas to conduct destructive cyber warfare against critical infrastructure providers on a global scale.

last_week_digest.title: FBI warns extortion hackers are visiting US law firms to steal data
last_week_digest.tldr: The Silent Ransom Group is actively targeting law firms through sophisticated social engineering, including phishing and rare in-person office visits to exfiltrate sensitive data. By masquerading as IT support to gain remote access, these attackers bypass traditional defences, threatening to leak confidential client information for extortion. This shift towards physical and social manipulation signals a growing industry challenge where attackers exploit legitimate tools to evade detection.

last_week_digest.title: Cybercriminals sail away with data from 6 million Carnival customers
last_week_digest.tldr: Carnival Corporation recently confirmed a significant data breach involving 5,995,277 customers following a successful social engineering attack on a single employee account. The incident, attributed to the ShinyHunters group, exposed sensitive loyalty program records including names and birth dates. This breach highlights the persistent vulnerability of large-scale global enterprises to targeted phishing, signalling that human-centric security failures remain a critical operational risk despite existing corporate cybersecurity investments.

last_week_digest.title: Chinese Hackers Exploit Iran War to Target Maritime and Energy Companies
last_week_digest.tldr: Chinese state-linked threat actors are aggressively exploiting Middle Eastern geopolitical instability to compromise maritime and energy sectors globally. ESET researchers report that these espionage campaigns, spanning from October 2025 to March 2026, align with Beijing’s strategic economic interests in infrastructure and advanced technology. This trend signals an intensifying shift where nation-states leverage regional conflicts to conduct industrial reconnaissance, forcing multinational organisations to bolster their defences against persistent, intelligence-driven cyber-espionage.

last_week_digest.title: Charter Communications data breach affects 4.9 million accounts
last_week_digest.tldr: The ShinyHunters extortion gang compromised Charter Communications, exposing personal data for 4.9 million accounts after a successful vishing attack. By breaching the company's Salesforce instance, attackers leaked names, contact details, and internal job titles. This incident highlights the persistent vulnerability of major telecommunications infrastructure to credential-based attacks, signalling a growing industry trend where threat actors leverage third-party platforms to bypass perimeter defences and extort global service providers.

last_week_digest.title: Cruise giant Carnival confirms data breach affecting nearly 6 million people
last_week_digest.tldr: Cruise giant Carnival has confirmed a significant data breach involving the theft of personal information belonging to nearly six million individuals, including passport and driver’s license details. Following a compromised employee account, the ShinyHunters hacking group exfiltrated sensitive records, exposing the firm to potential regulatory scrutiny and reputational damage. This incident signals an urgent industry-wide need to bolster identity management protocols against persistent, extortion-focused threat actors globally.

last_week_digest.title: Russia-Linked ‘GreyVibe’ Attackers Use AI to Supercharge Cyberattacks
last_week_digest.tldr: Russia-linked threat actor GreyVibe is now leveraging generative AI tools like ChatGPT and Gemini to significantly enhance the sophistication and scale of its cyberattacks. This operational shift enables more efficient reconnaissance and automated social engineering, posing severe risks to global corporate security. The adoption of AI by such groups signals a critical industry transition, forcing businesses to urgently reassess their defensive strategies against increasingly intelligent and persistent adversaries.

last_week_digest.title: Meet GREYVIBE, the Russia-Linked Hacking Group Using AI to Target Ukraine and Still Making Rookie Mistakes
last_week_digest.tldr: The Russia-linked group GREYVIBE has launched five distinct attack chains against Ukrainian military, government, and commercial entities since August 2025. By integrating AI tools like ChatGPT and Ideogram to generate malware and infrastructure, the group compensates for operational inexperience. This trend signals an evolving threat landscape where lower-skilled actors leverage artificial intelligence to execute persistent, complex campaigns, effectively blurring the lines between state-sponsored espionage and cybercriminal activity.

last_week_digest.title: ShinyHunters Leaks Charter Communications Data, Potentially Impacting 5 Million Customers
last_week_digest.tldr: The cybercriminal group ShinyHunters leaked data stolen from Charter Communications after a failed extortion attempt, exposing approximately 4.9 million customer records, including names, email addresses, and phone numbers. While the company confirmed the breach, it denied the theft of sensitive proprietary network information. This incident underscores the persistent threat of extortion-driven data leaks, signalling that large telecommunications providers remain primary targets for groups leveraging stolen credentials.

last_week_digest.title: Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN auth bypass flaw now exploited in attacks
last_week_digest.tldr: Palo Alto Networks confirmed active exploitation of a critical authentication bypass vulnerability, CVE-2026-0257, within its GlobalProtect VPN software. Attackers are leveraging forged cookies to gain unauthorised network access, prompting CISA to mandate urgent patching. This incident highlights significant operational risks for global enterprises relying on VPN gateways, signalling that attackers are increasingly targeting identity-based authentication mechanisms to circumvent traditional perimeter defences and compromise internal corporate infrastructure at scale.

last_week_digest.title: 23andMe inherits lawsuit over 'disturbing' DNA data breach
last_week_digest.tldr: California regulators are suing 23andMe following a 2023 breach that exposed the sensitive genetic data of nearly seven million users. Although the initial intrusion targeted only 14,000 accounts, systemic security failures allowed widespread unauthorised access. This litigation highlights the severe operational risks of mishandling highly personal information, signalling that regulators are increasingly prioritising corporate accountability and transparency regarding incident response protocols for firms managing sensitive global consumer datasets.

last_week_digest.title: In Other News: Trump Mobile Data Breach, FIFA World Cup Phishing, CISA Responds to Supply Chain Attacks
last_week_digest.tldr: Trump Mobile recently suffered a data breach exposing sensitive customer information, highlighting persistent vulnerabilities in telecommunications infrastructure. Simultaneously, malicious actors are leveraging the 2026 FIFA World Cup to launch sophisticated phishing campaigns, while CISA addresses mounting risks within global supply chains. These developments signal a critical shift, as attackers increasingly exploit high-profile events and systemic third-party dependencies, forcing organisations to prioritise robust data protection and proactive threat intelligence.

last_week_digest.title: Attackers Abuse Shared Content for ChatGPT Phishing Campaign
last_week_digest.tldr: Threat actors are exploiting legitimate ChatGPT and Claude shared content features to distribute infostealer malware through sophisticated ClickFix campaigns. By leveraging trusted domains and conditional rendering to bypass automated security scanners, attackers increasingly use malvertising and SEO poisoning to target unsuspecting users. This shift signals a broader industry trend where adversaries weaponise AI-normalised installation workflows, forcing organisations to rethink security awareness regarding command-line execution and browser-based threats.

last_week_digest.title: Hackers hijack thousands of sites for ClickFix and FakeUpdate attacks
last_week_digest.tldr: The DriveSurge threat actor has compromised thousands of high-reputation websites to facilitate large-scale malware distribution via ClickFix and FakeUpdate social engineering tactics. By acting as an initial access broker, the group redirects unsuspecting visitors to malicious payloads, threatening both Windows and macOS environments. This campaign highlights a growing industry trend where attackers weaponise legitimate infrastructure, forcing organisations to reconsider the security of their digital supply chains globally.

last_week_digest.title: Afghan finance officials targeted by suspected Pakistani cyberespionage campaign
last_week_digest.tldr: The Pakistan-linked threat actor SideCopy has launched a sophisticated cyberespionage campaign targeting Afghanistan’s Ministry of Finance and provincial officials. By leveraging compromised government infrastructure to distribute XenoRAT malware via targeted phishing, attackers gained persistent access to sensitive state communications. This operation highlights a growing trend of regional adversaries utilising highly tailored reconnaissance to bypass network defences, signalling an urgent need for enhanced state-level email security protocols globally.

last_week_digest.title: Red Hat npm packages compromised to steal developer credentials
last_week_digest.tldr: Attackers compromised 32 Red Hat npm packages, exposing 117,000 weekly downloads to the Miasma credential-stealing malware. By hijacking a developer’s GitHub account, threat actors injected malicious scripts to harvest cloud secrets, SSH keys, and CI/CD tokens. While Red Hat reports no impact on customer production systems, this incident highlights the persistent vulnerability of software supply chains, signalling that trusted developer ecosystems remain primary targets for sophisticated credential theft.

last_week_digest.title: Hackers duped Meta AI support chatbot to steal celebrity Instagram accounts
last_week_digest.tldr: Hackers exploited Meta’s AI support chatbot to hijack thousands of Instagram accounts by manipulating email recovery processes, resulting in the theft of high-value handles worth over $1 million. This incident highlights the severe operational risks of integrating large language models with elevated system permissions. It signals a critical industry shift, demonstrating that deploying AI agents without robust, deterministic security guardrails creates dangerous vulnerabilities for global platform integrity.

last_week_digest.title: China Uses Dual-Method Cyberattack on Czech Orgs
last_week_digest.tldr: Chinese threat actors are targeting Czech and Taiwanese government, academic, and financial sectors through the Operation Dragon Weave spear-phishing campaign. Utilizing dual-method Azureveil malware, these attacks facilitate sensitive data exfiltration. This trend signals that geopolitical tensions are increasingly driving sophisticated, multi-stage cyber espionage against high-value European and Asian institutional targets.

last_week_digest.title: Russia claims foreign spy agencies hacked officials' phones
last_week_digest.tldr: Russia’s Federal Security Service alleges foreign intelligence agencies conducted a large-scale espionage operation, compromising mobile devices of senior officials to extract sensitive communications and geolocation data. This incident signals a growing trend of state-sponsored actors weaponising global IT infrastructure, forcing international technology firms to confront increasing geopolitical scrutiny and liability.

last_week_digest.title: VS Code zero-day lets hackers steal GitHub tokens in one click
last_week_digest.tldr: A newly disclosed zero-day vulnerability in Visual Studio Code allows attackers to steal GitHub OAuth tokens via malicious extensions, granting unauthorised access to private repositories. This flaw highlights critical supply chain risks for developers and signals growing industry frustration regarding Microsoft’s handling of security disclosures and its opaque patch management processes.

last_week_digest.title: Over 116,000 Minecraft systems infected in WeedHack malware campaign
last_week_digest.tldr: The WeedHack malware campaign has compromised over 116,000 Minecraft systems globally since January via malicious mods and SEO poisoning. This Malware-as-a-Service operation steals credentials and financial data, signalling a growing trend where accessible, low-cost infostealers empower amateur actors to conduct large-scale cyberattacks, significantly increasing risks for individual users and digital platforms.

last_week_digest.title: Supply Chain Attack Hits 32 Red Hat NPM Packages
last_week_digest.tldr: Hackers recently compromised 32 Red Hat NPM packages, publishing 96 malicious versions containing credential-stealing malware. This supply chain breach threatens global software integrity, forcing organisations to urgently audit dependencies. The incident signals a growing industry trend where attackers exploit open-source ecosystems to infiltrate corporate environments, necessitating more rigorous automated security vetting.
